indicators.name: VIX (Fear Index)
indicators.what: The CBOE Volatility Index measures the market's expectation of 30-day S&P 500 volatility, derived from option prices. Often called the "fear gauge."
indicators.calc: Model-free variance swap formula aggregating prices of OTM S&P 500 puts and calls across two expiration dates bracketing 30 days: VIX = 100 * sqrt((2/T) * sum[(dK/K^2) * e^(rT) * Q(K)] - (1/T)*(F/K0 - 1)^2)

indicators.name: VVIX (Vol of VIX)
indicators.what: The CBOE VVIX measures the expected volatility of the VIX itself -- the "volatility of volatility." Spikes when options markets are uncertain about future fear levels.
indicators.calc: Same model-free variance swap methodology as VIX, but applied to VIX options instead of S&P 500 options.
indicators.direction: Higher = more uncertainty about volatility = danger

indicators.name: CBOE SKEW Index
indicators.what: The CBOE SKEW Index measures perceived tail risk by quantifying the skewness of S&P 500 returns implied by option prices. Higher values mean the market prices a greater probability of extreme left-tail moves.
indicators.calc: SKEW = 100 - 10 * S, where S is the implied third moment (skewness) extracted from OTM option prices. Baseline of 100 = log-normal returns (no skew).
indicators.direction: Higher = more tail risk priced in

indicators.name: Realized Volatility (21d)
indicators.what: The annualized standard deviation of daily S&P 500 log-returns over the past 22 trading days. Measures actual (not implied) market turbulence.
indicators.calc: RV = std(ln(P_t / P_{t-1}), window=22) * sqrt(252) * 100, where P is the S&P 500 daily close.

indicators.name: Variance Risk Premium
indicators.what: The Variance Risk Premium is the difference between implied variance (VIX^2) and realized variance. A positive VRP is normal (investors pay a premium for protection). When VRP turns negative, realized volatility has exceeded what was priced in -- the market was surprised.
indicators.calc: VRP = VIX^2 - RealizedVol(22d)^2. Positive = normal risk premium. Negative = realized > implied = danger.
indicators.source: Derived from VIX and S&P 500 returns
indicators.direction: Lower/negative = more danger (market surprised by vol)

indicators.name: VIX-Realized Vol Spread
indicators.what: The spread between VIX and realized volatility. A positive spread means implied vol exceeds realized (normal). A negative spread means realized vol has spiked above what was expected.
indicators.calc: Spread = VIX - RealizedVol(22d). Positive = cushion. Negative = market surprised.
indicators.source: Derived from VIX and S&P 500 returns

indicators.name: High-Yield Credit Spread
indicators.what: The option-adjusted spread on high-yield (junk) corporate bonds over Treasuries. Measures the extra yield investors demand to hold risky corporate debt. Widens sharply when credit risk rises.
indicators.calc: OAS = HY bond yield - comparable-maturity Treasury yield, adjusted for embedded options using interest rate models. Reported in basis points.
indicators.source: ICE BofA US High Yield Index (FRED: BAMLH0A0HYM2)

indicators.name: Investment-Grade Spread
indicators.what: The option-adjusted spread on investment-grade corporate bonds. Widens when even high-quality corporate borrowers face stress.
indicators.calc: Same OAS methodology as HY but applied to investment-grade (BBB- and above) bonds.
indicators.source: ICE BofA US Corporate Index (FRED: BAMLC0A0CM)

indicators.name: BBB Credit Spread
indicators.what: The spread on BBB-rated bonds -- the lowest investment-grade tier. These bonds are one notch above junk, making them sensitive to downgrade risk during downturns.
indicators.calc: OAS on BBB-rated corporate bonds vs. Treasuries.
indicators.source: ICE BofA BBB US Corporate Index (FRED: BAMLC0A4CBBB)

indicators.name: Commercial Paper Spread
indicators.what: The spread between 3-month financial commercial paper and 3-month Treasury bills. Measures short-term funding stress for financial institutions.
indicators.calc: CP Spread = 3-Month Financial CP Rate - 3-Month T-Bill Rate (both annualized yields).

indicators.name: HY Spread Momentum (1M)
indicators.what: The 21-day change in high-yield credit spreads. Rapidly widening spreads signal deteriorating credit conditions and often precede broader market stress.
indicators.calc: HY_OAS_Momentum = HY_OAS(t) - HY_OAS(t-21). Positive = spreads widening.
indicators.source: Derived from ICE BofA HY OAS

indicators.name: Excess Bond Premium
indicators.what: The Excess Bond Premium from Gilchrist & Zakrajsek (2012) decomposes corporate bond spreads into a component explained by firm fundamentals and a residual (the EBP) that captures investor risk appetite. Elevated EBP = risk aversion.
indicators.calc: Residual from regressing corporate bond spreads on firm-level default risk measures (distance-to-default, leverage, earnings). Requires firm-level bond data.

indicators.name: Chicago Fed NFCI
indicators.what: The Chicago Fed National Financial Conditions Index aggregates 105 measures of financial activity (money markets, debt, equity, banking, shadow banking). Zero = long-run average conditions.
indicators.calc: Principal component analysis on 105 weekly financial indicators, normalized so that zero = average and positive = tighter-than-average conditions.

indicators.name: KC Fed Stress Index
indicators.what: The Kansas City Fed Financial Stress Index measures the level of stress in U.S. financial markets using 11 variables including credit spreads, equity vol, and interbank lending.
indicators.calc: Principal component analysis on 11 financial market variables. Zero = long-run average; positive = above-average stress.

indicators.name: Yield Curve (10Y-2Y)
indicators.what: The spread between 10-year and 2-year Treasury yields. An inverted curve (negative spread) has preceded every U.S. recession since 1970, typically 12-18 months ahead.
indicators.calc: 10Y-2Y Spread = 10-Year Treasury Yield - 2-Year Treasury Yield. Negative = inverted.

indicators.name: Yield Curve (10Y-3M)
indicators.what: The spread between 10-year and 3-month Treasury yields. The NY Fed's preferred yield curve measure for recession forecasting. Arguably more reliable than 10Y-2Y.
indicators.calc: 10Y-3M Spread = 10-Year Treasury Yield - 3-Month Treasury Yield.

indicators.name: Fed Funds - 10Y Spread
indicators.what: The spread between the Fed Funds Rate and the 10-year Treasury yield. When the policy rate exceeds the long rate, monetary policy is restrictive -- historically associated with recession.
indicators.calc: FFR-10Y Spread = Effective Federal Funds Rate - 10-Year Treasury Yield. Positive = policy rate above long rate.

indicators.name: NY Fed Recession Prob
indicators.what: The NY Fed's estimated probability of recession within the next 12 months, based on the Estrella-Mishkin probit model using the yield curve spread.
indicators.calc: Published by the NY Fed. Based on the Estrella-Mishkin (1998) probit model: P(recession in 12M) = Phi(alpha + beta * Spread_10Y3M). The NY Fed periodically re-estimates the coefficients.
indicators.source: NY Fed via FRED: RECPROUSM156N (published monthly)

indicators.name: Initial Jobless Claims
indicators.what: Weekly initial unemployment insurance claims (4-week average), scaled by the civilian labor force. Rising claims signal deteriorating labor market conditions.
indicators.calc: (4-Week Average Initial Claims) / (Civilian Labor Force * 1000) * 100. Expressed as claims per 100 workers.
indicators.source: DOL via FRED: IC4WSA (4-wk avg), CLF16OV (labor force)

indicators.name: Sahm Rule
indicators.what: Claudia Sahm's real-time recession indicator. When the 3-month average unemployment rate rises 0.50 percentage points above its 12-month low, a recession has begun. Every trigger since 1970 has correctly identified recession.
indicators.calc: Sahm Rule = (3-month avg U-rate) - (min U-rate over prior 12 months). Trigger at >= 0.50 pp.

indicators.name: Unemployment Rate
indicators.what: The U.S. civilian unemployment rate from the Bureau of Labor Statistics. A lagging indicator of recessions but important for confirming economic weakness.
indicators.calc: (Number of unemployed / Civilian labor force) * 100. Based on the Current Population Survey (CPS), ~60,000 households monthly.

indicators.name: Nonfarm Payrolls Momentum
indicators.what: The 3-month rolling average of monthly changes in nonfarm payrolls. Smooths noisy monthly jobs reports. Declining momentum signals the labor market is losing steam.
indicators.calc: NFP_Mom = rolling_mean(diff(PAYEMS), 3 months). PAYEMS is total nonfarm payrolls in thousands.

indicators.name: Durable Goods Orders YoY
indicators.what: Year-over-year growth in new orders for durable goods (goods lasting 3+ years). A leading indicator of business investment and economic health.
indicators.calc: YoY% = (DGORDER(t) / DGORDER(t-12) - 1) * 100. DGORDER is monthly total durable goods orders ($M, seasonally adjusted).

indicators.name: Industrial Production YoY
indicators.what: Year-over-year growth in the Federal Reserve's Industrial Production Index, measuring output of manufacturing, mining, and utilities. Negative growth has historically accompanied every recession.
indicators.calc: YoY% = (INDPRO(t) / INDPRO(t-12) - 1) * 100. INDPRO is an index (2017=100).

indicators.name: Housing Permits YoY
indicators.what: Year-over-year growth in new housing building permits. Housing is a leading sector -- permit declines of 20%+ have preceded every recession since 1960.
indicators.calc: YoY% = (PERMIT(t) / PERMIT(t-12) - 1) * 100. PERMIT is monthly new private housing units authorized (thousands, SAAR).

indicators.name: Philly Fed Manufacturing
indicators.what: The Philadelphia Fed Manufacturing Business Outlook Survey diffusion index. A reading above 0 indicates expansion. One of the oldest and most-watched regional manufacturing surveys.
indicators.calc: Diffusion Index = (% reporting increase) - (% reporting decrease) for general business activity. Range roughly -40 to +50.

indicators.name: UMich Consumer Sentiment
indicators.what: The University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index. Based on a monthly survey of ~500 households about their financial conditions and economic outlook. A leading indicator of consumer spending.
indicators.calc: Composite index from 5 survey questions about personal finances (current and expected) and business conditions (short-run and long-run), plus buying conditions. Base period 1966 = 100.
indicators.source: University of Michigan via FRED: UMCSENT

indicators.name: M2 Money Supply Growth
indicators.what: Year-over-year growth in M2 money supply (cash, checking, savings, small CDs, money market funds). Negative M2 growth is extremely rare and signals severe liquidity contraction.
indicators.calc: YoY% = (M2(t) / M2(t-12) - 1) * 100. M2 is in billions of dollars, seasonally adjusted.

indicators.name: S&P 500 vs 200-DMA
indicators.what: The percentage distance of the S&P 500 from its 200-day moving average. A widely used trend indicator -- below the 200-DMA is considered bearish.
indicators.calc: % vs 200-DMA = (S&P500 / SMA_200 - 1) * 100. Positive = above trend. Negative = below trend.

indicators.name: Death Cross (50/200 MA)
indicators.what: A binary technical signal: 1 when the 50-day moving average crosses below the 200-day moving average. Widely cited as a bearish signal in financial media.
indicators.calc: Death Cross = 1 if SMA_50 < SMA_200, else 0. (Stored as 0 or 100 in the model.)

indicators.name: RSI (14-day)
indicators.what: The Relative Strength Index (14-day), a momentum oscillator measuring the speed and magnitude of recent price changes. Above 70 = overbought (vulnerable to correction), below 30 = oversold.
indicators.calc: RSI = 100 - 100/(1+RS), where RS = EMA(gains, 14) / EMA(losses, 14). Uses Wilder's smoothing (alpha = 1/14).
indicators.direction: Extreme values (both high and low) = danger

indicators.name: 12-1 Month Momentum
indicators.what: The Jegadeesh-Titman 12-1 month momentum: the S&P 500 return from 12 months ago to 1 month ago, excluding the most recent month. Negative momentum has historically preceded further declines.
indicators.calc: Mom_12_1 = (P_{t-21} / P_{t-252} - 1) * 100. Skips the last 21 trading days to avoid short-term reversal.

indicators.name: Trailing 1Y Drawdown
indicators.what: The current S&P 500 drawdown from its trailing 252-day (1-year) high. Measures how far the market has fallen from its recent peak.
indicators.calc: DD = (P_t / max(P_{t-252:t}) - 1) * 100. Always <= 0. Zero = at the 1-year high.

indicators.name: Buffett Indicator
indicators.what: Warren Buffett's "single best measure of where valuations stand": total stock market capitalization as a percentage of GDP. Persistently high readings suggest overvaluation.
indicators.calc: Buffett Indicator = (Total US Market Cap / GDP) * 100. Uses Wilshire 5000 Full Cap Index (FRED: WILL5000IND), where at inception 1 point = $1B market cap, divided by nominal GDP.

indicators.name: Household Equity Allocation
indicators.what: Household equity allocation from the Fed's Flow of Funds (Z.1) report. Measures the share of household financial assets allocated to equities. High allocation = late-cycle euphoria.
indicators.calc: Direct percentage from Fed Z.1 table. Represents corporate equities as a share of total household financial assets.
indicators.source: Federal Reserve Flow of Funds (FRED: BOGZ1FL153064486Q)
indicators.direction: Higher = more crowded into stocks = danger

indicators.name: FINRA Margin Debt
indicators.what: Total debit balances in customers' securities margin accounts at FINRA member broker-dealers, scaled by GDP. Measures the total amount of borrowed money used to buy stocks relative to the size of the economy.
indicators.calc: Margin Debt / GDP = (FINRA Debit Balances in $M / 1000) / (GDP in $B) * 100. Expressed as a percentage.

indicators.name: Margin Debt YoY Growth
indicators.what: Year-over-year growth in raw FINRA margin debt (debit balances). Rapid leverage growth signals accelerating speculation. Sharp declines can signal forced deleveraging.
indicators.calc: YoY% = (MarginDebt(t) / MarginDebt(t-12) - 1) * 100. Computed on raw monthly dollar values.

indicators.name: CFTC Leveraged Net Short
indicators.what: Net positioning of leveraged money (hedge funds, CTAs) in S&P 500 futures from the CFTC Commitments of Traders report. When "smart money" is heavily net short, it can signal informed bearishness.
indicators.calc: Net Long = %OI Long (Leveraged) - %OI Short (Leveraged). Expressed as percentage of total open interest.
indicators.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders, Financial Futures (weekly)
indicators.direction: More net short by smart money = danger

indicators.name: CFTC Asset Mgr Net Long
indicators.what: Net positioning of asset managers (pensions, mutual funds, insurance) in S&P 500 futures. Extreme net long positioning by "slow money" can signal crowding and vulnerability to unwind.
indicators.calc: Net Long = %OI Long (Asset Mgr) - %OI Short (Asset Mgr). Expressed as percentage of total open interest.
indicators.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders, Financial Futures (weekly)

indicators.name: Gold / S&P 500 Ratio
indicators.what: The ratio of gold price to S&P 500. Rising ratio signals flight from risk assets to safe havens. Gold outperforms equities during stress periods.
indicators.calc: Gold/SPX Ratio = Gold Futures Price / S&P 500 Index Level.
indicators.direction: Higher = more flight to safety = danger

indicators.name: Copper / Gold Ratio
indicators.what: The copper-to-gold ratio. Copper ("Dr. Copper") is pro-cyclical (industrial demand), while gold is counter-cyclical (safe haven). A declining ratio signals deteriorating growth expectations.
indicators.calc: Cu/Au Ratio = Copper Futures Price / Gold Futures Price.

indicators.name: US Dollar Index
indicators.what: The US Dollar Index measures the value of the dollar against a basket of 6 major currencies (EUR 57.6%, JPY 13.6%, GBP 11.9%, CAD 9.1%, SEK 4.2%, CHF 3.6%). A strong dollar tightens global financial conditions.
indicators.calc: Geometric weighted average of USD exchange rates vs. 6 currencies: DXY = 50.14348112 * prod(FX_i ^ w_i). Base March 1973 = 100.
indicators.direction: Higher = stronger dollar = global tightening = danger

indicators.name: Fed Funds Rate
indicators.what: The effective federal funds rate -- the interest rate at which banks lend reserves to each other overnight. The primary tool of Federal Reserve monetary policy.
indicators.calc: Volume-weighted median rate on overnight federal funds transactions reported by major brokers. Set by the FOMC within a target range.

indicators.name: Fed Reverse Repo YoY
indicators.what: Year-over-year change in the Fed's Overnight Reverse Repo Facility (ON RRP) usage. The ON RRP absorbs excess liquidity. Declining usage = liquidity draining from the system.
indicators.calc: YoY% = (ON_RRP(t) / ON_RRP(t-252) - 1) * 100. Uses 252 business days for daily data.

indicators.name: Bank Lending Standards
indicators.what: The Federal Reserve's Senior Loan Officer Opinion Survey on bank lending practices. Net percentage of banks tightening standards on C&I loans. Sustained tightening has preceded every recession since 1990.
indicators.calc: Net % Tightening = (% banks reporting tightened standards) - (% reporting eased standards). Positive = net tightening.
indicators.source: Federal Reserve Board quarterly survey (FRED: DRTSCILM)
